items.q: What is the correct form of 'parler' (to speak) for 'nous'?
items.why: Students apply the same ending across persons. ER verbs use different endings: je parle, tu parles, il parle, NOUS parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent. Each subject takes its own ending.

items.q: Which verbs take 'être' as their auxiliary in the passé composé?
items.correct: Movement/state-change verbs (DR & MRS VAN DER TRAMP) and all reflexive verbs.
items.why: Students forget reflexives. Both groups use être: aller/venir/monter, etc. AND every reflexive verb (je me suis lavé). With être, the past participle agrees with the subject.

items.q: Is 'le problème' masculine or feminine?
items.why: Students use 'ends in -e = feminine' as a rule. Many exceptions exist: le problème, le système, le programme, le poème - all masculine despite the -e ending. Gender must often be memorised.

items.q: What is the feminine plural form of 'beau'?
items.why: Students apply default endings to irregular adjectives. Beau has irregular forms: beau (m), belle (f), beaux (m pl), BELLES (f pl). The feminine takes belle, then adds -s.

items.q: Where does 'petit' usually go in relation to its noun?
items.misconception: After the noun like most French adjectives.
items.random_wrong: Either position with the same meaning.
items.why: Students apply 'all French adjectives follow nouns' too rigidly. BAGS adjectives (Beauty, Age, Goodness, Size) typically PRECEDE. Petit (size) and bon (goodness) come before.

items.q: How do you say 'I do not eat' in French?
items.why: Students forget the verb 'sandwich'. Ne and pas wrap AROUND the verb in present tense - ne BEFORE the verb, pas AFTER. In compound tenses, they wrap around the auxiliary.

items.q: What does 'actuellement' mean in French?
items.why: A classic faux ami. Actuellement looks like 'actually' but means CURRENTLY. To say 'actually' use en fait or en réalité. These false friends regularly cost marks.

items.q: What does 'librairie' mean?
items.why: Students assume librairie = library. It actually means BOOKSHOP. The French word for library is bibliothèque. Both relate to books but serve different purposes.

items.q: Which letter is typically SILENT at the end of French words like 'petit' or 'froid'?
items.why: Students try to pronounce every letter. Final consonants in French are usually silent (CaReFuL exceptions: c, r, f, l). Petit is pronounced 'puh-TEE', not 'puh-TEET'.

items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the passé composé?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.random_wrong: When you don't know what tense to use.
items.why: Students confuse past tenses by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: passé composé = completed event ('I went'); imparfait = ongoing/habitual/descriptive ('I used to go', 'I was going').

items.q: What follows 'il faut que' grammatically?
items.correct: A verb in the subjunctive mood.
items.why: Students use infinitive after impersonal expressions. 'Il faut que tu PARTES' (subjunctive), not 'il faut que tu partir'. The que triggers subjunctive in the following clause.

items.q: What is the difference between 'le' and 'lui' as pronouns?
items.correct: Le replaces a direct object (him/it); lui replaces an indirect object (to him/her).
items.random_wrong: Le is masculine, lui is feminine.
items.why: Students don't distinguish object types. 'Je le vois' (I see him - direct); 'Je lui parle' (I speak TO him - indirect). The verb's preposition determines the pronoun type.

items.q: What tense follows 'depuis' for ongoing situations in French?
items.correct: Present tense (J'habite ici DEPUIS 5 ans - I have lived here for 5 years).
items.misconception: Past tense (because it is translated as 'have lived' in English).
items.why: Students translate the English tense literally. French uses PRESENT with depuis because the action is ongoing - 'I am still living'. The English perfect tense misleads students into using past in French.

items.q: What follows 'si' (if) in a conditional sentence like 'If I were rich, I would travel'?
items.correct: Imperfect tense (Si j'ÉTAIS riche, je voyagerais).
items.why: A classic error - students put conditional after si. The rule: si + IMPERFECT in the if-clause, conditional in the result clause. Never 'si je serais' - always 'si j'étais'.

items.q: How is the 'on' in 'bon' pronounced?
items.correct: As a nasal vowel - the n is not fully pronounced.
items.why: Students pronounce nasal vowels like English consonants. French 'on', 'an', 'in', 'un' are nasalised vowels - air resonates through the nose but the n consonant itself isn't articulated.

items.q: How do you say 'I like coffee' in French?
items.why: English drops articles for general likes/dislikes; French keeps them. For general preferences, use the DEFINITE article: j'aime le chocolat, je déteste les épinards.

items.q: How do you say 'my house' in French?
items.why: Students use mon as default. French possessives agree with the OBJECT's gender, not the owner. Maison is feminine, so MA maison. (Note: mon goes before feminine nouns starting with a vowel - mon amie.)

items.q: What is the 'vous' form of 'être' in the present tense?
items.why: Students apply -ez ending from regular verbs. Être is irregular: je suis, tu es, il est, nous sommes, vous ÊTES, ils sont. Memorisation is essential - être appears constantly.

items.q: How do you say 'I am eating bread' (some bread) in French?
items.random_wrong: Je mange le pain (which means 'the bread').
items.why: Students drop the partitive. French requires DU/DE LA/DES for 'some' even when English omits it. Skipping it makes the sentence ungrammatical in French.

items.q: How do you say 'in France'?
items.why: Students default to à. The rule: EN for feminine countries (most ending -e: France, Italie, Espagne); AU for masculine (au Portugal, au Canada); AUX for plural (aux États-Unis). À is for cities (à Paris).

items.q: What is the most common informal way to form a yes/no question in French?
items.correct: Rising intonation (statement said as a question) or using 'est-ce que'.
items.why: Students learn formal inversion first. In conversation, French uses intonation or est-ce que far more often. Inversion sounds formal/written - 'Tu parles français?' is the everyday form.

items.q: What does 'sensible' mean in French?
items.why: Sensible is a faux ami. French 'sensible' = sensitive/easily affected. English 'sensible' = raisonnable in French. Confusion leads to wrong impressions of someone's character.

items.q: When does the past participle agree with avoir verbs?
items.correct: When the direct object comes BEFORE the verb (la pomme que j'ai mangée).
items.why: Students learn 'avoir = no agreement' as a blanket rule. The exception is PRECEDING DIRECT OBJECT: 'Je l'ai mangée' (la pomme) requires the participle to agree with the object pronoun.

items.q: What does 'mes parents' mean in French?
items.correct: My parents (mother and father specifically) - but 'parents' can also mean relatives more broadly.
items.misconception: It always means all my relatives.
items.why: Students think French 'parents' = English 'relatives' because in some contexts it does. Usually 'mes parents' = mother+father; 'mes proches' or 'ma famille' = relatives more broadly. Context matters.

items.q: When meeting a teacher or stranger for the first time, do you use 'tu' or 'vous'?
items.misconception: Tu, because it's singular and you're talking to one person.
items.random_wrong: Either is fine in modern French.
items.why: Students see 'vous' as plural only. In French, 'vous' is BOTH plural AND the formal singular. Using 'tu' with a stranger or authority figure is rude. Wait to be invited to 'tutoyer'.

items.q: How do you say 'I study maths' in French?
items.why: Students drop the article like English. School subjects in French take the DEFINITE article: 'j'aime le français', 'j'étudie les sciences', 'je déteste l'histoire'. This is the rule for general nouns.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to Spain' in French?
items.why: Students confuse aller (to go - movement away from speaker) and venir (to come - movement towards speaker). 'Je vais' = I'm going; 'je viens' = I'm coming. Also: en + feminine country, au + masculine, aux + plural.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Paris' (near future) in French?
items.why: Students add 'à' between aller and the infinitive. Futur proche structure is ALLER (conjugated) + INFINITIVE, with no preposition: 'je vais manger', 'je vais aller', 'je vais partir'.

items.q: How do you say 'I play the piano' vs 'I play football'?
items.correct: Je joue DU piano (instrument) vs je joue AU football (sport).
items.misconception: Same preposition for both - 'je joue piano', 'je joue football'.
items.random_wrong: Je joue le piano, je joue le football.
items.why: Students drop or confuse prepositions. The rule: jouer DE + instrument (de, du, de la); jouer À + sport/game (à, au, aux). Easy way to lose marks in speaking exams about hobbies.

items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: How do you say 'Currently, I am using social media' in French?
items.why: Students avoid actuellement thinking it means 'actually'. It really means CURRENTLY. Useful for tech/social media topics. To say 'actually' (in reality), use 'en fait' or 'en réalité'.

items.q: What is the French word for 'Christmas'?
items.why: Students omit the diaeresis (tréma). Many festival words have crucial accents: Noël, fête, pâques, élève (student-related). Omitting accents in writing loses marks; the tréma indicates 'e' is pronounced separately.

items.q: Which mood follows 'je souhaite que' (I wish that)?
items.why: Students use indicative after expressions of wish. SOUHAITER QUE, VOULOIR QUE, AIMER QUE all trigger subjunctive when there's a different subject. Note: espérer que typically takes indicative - a common irregularity.

items.q: How do you describe weather in the past, e.g. 'it was sunny'?
items.misconception: Passé composé - 'Il a fait beau' (which suggests a completed event).
items.why: Weather descriptions in past narratives use IMPERFECT (ongoing state/description). Passé composé is for completed events. 'Quand je suis arrivé, il faisait beau' = ongoing weather backdrop; arrived = completed action.

items.q: How do you say 'his sister' and 'her sister' in French?
items.correct: Both are 'sa sœur' - possessives agree with the OBJECT possessed, not the owner.
items.misconception: His = son, her = sa - based on owner's gender.
items.why: Students apply English logic where 'his/her' shows owner's gender. In French, the possessive agrees with what is owned: sœur is feminine, so SA regardless of whether owner is male or female. Context shows whose.

items.q: How do you say 'I was using my computer when the phone rang' in French?
items.correct: J'utilisais mon ordinateur quand le téléphone a sonné.
items.misconception: J'ai utilisé mon ordinateur quand le téléphone a sonné.
items.random_wrong: J'utilise mon ordinateur quand le téléphone sonne.
items.why: Students use passé composé for both verbs. The rule: ONGOING action (was using) = IMPARFAIT; INTERRUPTING action (rang) = PASSÉ COMPOSÉ. This tense pairing is critical for narrative writing.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.
